quote: Hi. This is Joe with Ion. Joe Joe, hi. This is Lisa Chris Smith. Kevin called and left me a message.
stewart_read: The rep used no Ion intro template — no Tesla framing, no form-fill reference, no state mention, no 'how are you doing' pause. The call opens as a callback on a garbled number, which creates lead-origin ambiguity (schema lead_types_and_bill_collection_rules requires lead type to interpret bill-collection effort correctly). The rep never establishes which intro variant applies, which means legitimacy is not anchored in the first 30 seconds as the schema requires.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: the rep may have been adapting to an unusual callback situation (cut-off number, Kevin's message) and chose to let Lisa lead. Rejected — the schema marks intro as hard_required regardless of call origin, and the rep had enough information to use the standard intro template.
coaching_implication: Pull this opening with the rep. The intro beat is hard_required and was skipped entirely. Ask the rep: what intro variant would you use on a callback where the lead origin is unclear? The answer should be standard intro + 'you filled out a form / were referred to us' — not a cold name exchange. Also flag: lead type was never established, which means the bill-collection effort later in the call cannot be correctly interpreted without that context.

quote: Well, what would if I had a shed and wanted to put solar on a shed, would that suffice?
stewart_read: Lisa's shed question is a secondary-structure solar signal. The rep dismissed it on structural grounds ('the panels would be a bit too heavy for that') without routing to a specialist or flagging it as a potential cross-sell. The schema's cross_sell_signals section marks solar_for_secondary_structure as TBD but the routing logic is identical to the roofing cross-sell: don't dismiss, bridge to the specialist who can evaluate.
alternative_hypothesis: Reactive read: the rep may be correct that a shed can't hold panels, making the dismissal factually accurate. Partially accepted — but even a factually accurate dismissal should end with 'our specialist can confirm that when they come out' rather than a hard no that closes the door.
coaching_implication: Use this moment to introduce the secondary-structure routing concept. The rep's instinct to answer the question directly is understandable but costs a potential cross-sell signal. Suggested redirect: 'That's a great question — our specialist will actually be able to look at the whole property when they come out and tell you what's feasible.' This keeps the door open without fabricating capability.

quote: the solar bill that I had was through Sunrun Solar, and the bill was $250 a month. Yet, I still had a $63 electric bill from the electric company. So there lies in the the non trust issue with me
stewart_read: This is a prior-bad-experience trust objection, not a vulnerability disclosure. Lisa is citing a specific, named company (Sunrun) and a specific dollar outcome as evidence that the rep's billing claim is unreliable. The rep's response at 05:57 — conceding partial offset is possible — validates her skepticism rather than reframing it. The inversion available here: Sunrun's outcome is evidence of why the design-first approach matters, not evidence that solar doesn't work. The rep had the material to make that move ('that's exactly why we build the design before we make any promises') but didn't.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: the rep may have conceded partial offset to appear honest and build credibility before the appointment. Rejected — the concession came without any bridge to the appointment as the venue where the actual numbers would be established, leaving Lisa with a confirmed doubt and no resolution path.
coaching_implication: This is the highest-leverage missed reframe on the call. Walk the rep through the inversion: Lisa's Sunrun experience is not an argument against solar — it's an argument for doing the design correctly. The rep should have said something like: 'That's exactly why we don't make promises before we see your numbers — Sunrun's outcome tells me they didn't design it right for your home. That's what the appointment is for.' No fabricated numbers needed; the reframe uses Lisa's own experience as the reason to proceed.

quote: Sometimes we're not able to offset it a 100%. Sometimes we're only able to cover, you know, half of the usage.
stewart_read: This is the moment the rep's own framing breaks down. Lisa drove the billing education exchange across three turns (03:40, 04:31, 05:18) — the rep was responding to her questions, not volunteering scope-creep content. But at this specific line, the rep crosses from defending the goal to conceding the exception, and does so without the escape hatch. The schema's universal escape hatch ('That's exactly the kind of question our specialist is built to answer') was available here and would have ended the educational loop while preserving the appointment's credibility.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: the rep may have been using honesty about partial offset to build trust before the appointment. Rejected — the concession was made without any bridge, leaving Lisa with a confirmed doubt and no forward path. Honesty without a bridge is just a concession.
coaching_implication: Show the rep where the escape hatch applies. The rep held up well across two turns of Lisa's billing math; the failure point is this specific line. Suggested pivot: 'Look, I want to be straight with you — the exact numbers depend on your home's usage and sun exposure, which is exactly what the specialist will calculate. That's the only way to know what your actual outcome would be.' This is honest AND redirects to the appointment.

quote: Like, about how much is your typical bill with them? $2.41.
stewart_read: The rep captures the bill anchor ($241/month) but does not flip it. Lisa has already expressed frustration with North Neck ('It's ridiculous') at 07:29 — the emotional alignment-against-utility is right there, handed to the rep. The schema's capture pattern is 'ask + acknowledge + flip'; the rep does ask and acknowledge but drops the flip entirely, moving immediately to bill collection logistics.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: the rep may have been saving the flip for after bill collection, once the kWh data was in hand. Partially plausible — but Lisa's frustration at 07:29 ('Yeah. It's ridiculous') is a live emotional signal that the rep lets pass without anchoring. The flip doesn't require kWh data; it requires the customer's own words.
coaching_implication: This is the universal floor-wide gap the schema identifies. Show the rep Lisa's 'It's ridiculous' line and ask: what would you say next if you wanted to make that feeling work for you? The answer is the flip: 'Exactly — and that's the whole point. You're already paying $241 a month to them. The question is whether we can get you a better deal on the same electricity.' No fabricated numbers; Lisa's own bill is the anchor.

quote: And then we can have somebody, you know, stop by and go over the design with you.
stewart_read: The rep books the appointment but the button-up is missing four of six required elements. Present: appointment datetime confirmed (02:30 tomorrow, 13:54) and text follow-up promised (13:30). Missing: one-hour block stated, phone appointment specified, no-driving/at-computer anchor, and 'is that fair' verbal handshake. Additionally, 'stop by' implies an in-person visit, which contradicts the phone appointment protocol — this may create a no-show if Lisa expects someone at her door rather than a phone call.
coaching_implication: Run the rep through the six button-up elements as a checklist against this call. The 'stop by' language is the most urgent fix — if Lisa is expecting an in-person visit and gets a phone call, the sit is at risk before it starts. The 'is that fair' handshake and the no-driving/at-computer anchor are the two elements with the highest no-show correlation per the schema. Both were skipped.

quote: Even though I get sun all directions, I'd rather have it on the back.
stewart_read: Lisa's disclosure that she gets sun in all directions is a strong site-qualification signal that the rep walks past. This is not a constraint — it's the opposite. A home with full sun exposure in all directions is an ideal solar candidate. The rep's response ('That's a good deal') at 01:52 is directed at the new roof, not at the sun exposure signal. The rep never anchors on this as a positive qualifier.
coaching_implication: Show the rep this line as an example of an enthusiasm signal that looks like a constraint conversation. Lisa is telling the rep her home is a great solar candidate while framing it as a placement preference. The rep should have anchored: 'Full sun in all directions is actually ideal — that's exactly the kind of home where the numbers work best. The specialist will show you what the back-roof design looks like.' This would have shifted the energy from constraint-management to possibility-building.
